title: Charmed Life - Chapter 13 (Fragment 2)
text:
At last—very long last—it was time for Mary to bring the milk and biscuits, which she did, with a very portentous look. Crouched on the tray beside Mr. Saunders’ cup of coffee was a large, wet-looking, brown thing. Cat’s stomach seemed to leave him and take a plunge into the Castle cellars. From the look of Janet, hers was doing the same.

“What have you got there?” said Mr. Saunders.

“Gwendolen’s good deed for today,” Mary said grimly. “It’s Euphemia. Look at its face.”

Mr. Saunders bent and looked. Then he whirled around on Janet so fiercely that Janet half got out of her seat. “So that’s what you were laughing about!”

“I didn’t do it!” said Janet.

“Euphemia was in Gwendolen’s room, shut in the wardrobe, croaking her poor head off,” said Mary.

“I think this calls for Chrestomanci,” Mr. Saunders said. He strode towards the door.

The door opened before he got there and Chrestomanci himself came in, cheerful and busy, with some papers in one hand. “Michael,” he said, “have I caught you at the right—?” He stopped when he saw Mr. Saunders’ face. “Is something wrong?”

“Will you please to look at this frog, sir,” said Mary. “It was in Gwendolen’s wardrobe.”

Chrestomanci was wearing an exquisite gray suit with faint lilac stripes to it. He held his lilac silk cravat out of the way and bent to inspect the frog. Euphemia lifted her head and croaked at him beseechingly. There was a moment of ice-cold silence. It was a moment such as Cat hoped never to live through again. “Bless my soul!” Chrestomanci said, gently as frost freezes a window. “It’s Eugenia.”

“Euphemia, Daddy,” said Julia.

“Euphemia,” said Chrestomanci. “Of course. Now who did this?” Cat wondered how such a mild voice could send the hair pricking upright at the back of his head.

“Gwendolen, sir,” said Mary.

But Chrestomanci shook his smooth black head. “No. Don’t give a dog a bad name. It couldn’t have been Gwendolen. Michael took her witchcraft away last night.”

“Oh,” said Mr. Saunders, rather red in the face. “Stupid of me!”

“So who could it have been?” Chrestomanci wondered.

There was another freezing silence. It seemed to Cat about as long as an Ice Age. During it, Julia began to smile. She drummed her fingers on her desk and looked meditatively at Janet. Janet saw her and jumped. She drew in her breath sharply. Cat panicked. He was sure Janet was going to say what Gwendolen had done. He said the only thing he could think of to stop her.

“I did it,” he said loudly.

Cat could hardly bear the way they all looked at him. Julia was disgusted, Roger astonished. Mr. Saunders was fiercely angry. Mary looked at him as if he was a frog himself. But Chrestomanci was politely incredulous, and he was worst of all. “I beg your pardon, Eric,” he said. “This was you ?”

Cat stared at him with a strange misty wetness around his eyes. He thought it was due to terror. “It was a mistake,” he said. “I was trying a spell. I—I didn’t expect it to work. And then—and then Euphemia came in and turned into a frog. Just like that,” he explained.

Chrestomanci said, “But you were told not to practice magic on your own.”

“I know.” Cat hung his head, without having to pretend. “But I knew it wouldn’t work. Only it did, of course,” he explained.

vocabulary.definition: Very surprised or amazed.
vocabulary.example: Roger was astonished.

vocabulary.definition: To lean or incline the body forward or downwards.
vocabulary.example: He bent to inspect the frog.

vocabulary.definition: In a pleading or begging manner.
vocabulary.example: Euphemia lifted her head and croaked at him beseechingly.

vocabulary.definition: Uttered a deep, rough sound typical for frogs or worn voices.
vocabulary.example: Euphemia lifted her head and croaked at him beseechingly.

vocabulary.definition: Making a deep, hoarse sound, like a frog.
vocabulary.example: Euphemia was in Gwendolen’s room, shut in the wardrobe, croaking her poor head off.

vocabulary.definition: To bend down low and draw the body close to the ground, often to hide or prepare to spring.
vocabulary.example: Crouched on the tray beside Mr. Saunders’ cup of coffee was a large, wet-looking, brown thing.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or expressing strong dislike or disapproval.
vocabulary.example: Julia was disgusted.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely beautiful and delicate; of exceptional quality.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci was wearing an exquisite gray suit with faint lilac stripes.

vocabulary.definition: Lacking strength or brightness; barely perceptible.
vocabulary.example: Exquisite gray suit with faint lilac stripes.

vocabulary.definition: In a violent or intense manner; with strong and intense force or emotion.
vocabulary.example: He whirled around on Janet so fiercely that Janet half got out of her seat.

vocabulary.definition: In a stern or forbidding manner; showing seriousness or disapproval.
vocabulary.example: Mary said grimly, “It’s Euphemia.”

vocabulary.definition: Unwilling or unable to believe something; sceptical.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci was politely incredulous.

vocabulary.definition: To look at carefully in order to assess condition or discover details.
vocabulary.example: He bent to inspect the frog.

vocabulary.definition: An action or judgment that is misguided or wrong.
vocabulary.example: It was a mistake.

vocabulary.definition: Full of mist or seeming unclear, often because of emotion or moisture in the eyes.
vocabulary.example: Cat stared at him with a strange misty wetness around his eyes.

vocabulary.definition: A brief period of time.
vocabulary.example: There was a moment of ice-cold silence.

vocabulary.definition: To jump or dive quickly and energetically; to fall or move suddenly forward or downward.
vocabulary.example: Cat’s stomach seemed to leave him and take a plunge into the Castle cellars.

vocabulary.definition: Ominous, foreboding, or significant in a way that suggests something important or momentous is going to happen.
vocabulary.example: She did it with a very portentous look.

vocabulary.definition: To perform an activity or exercise repeatedly to improve skill.
vocabulary.example: You were told not to practice magic on your own.

vocabulary.definition: To behave as if something is true when it is not.
vocabulary.example: Without having to pretend.

vocabulary.definition: Complete absence of sound.
vocabulary.example: There was a moment of ice-cold silence.

vocabulary.definition: A neckband, predecessor to the modern tie, typically made of silk.
vocabulary.example: He held his lilac silk cravat out of the way.

vocabulary.definition: Having an even and regular surface; free from difficulties or obstacles.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci shook his smooth black head.

vocabulary.definition: Showing a lack of intelligence or common sense.
vocabulary.example: Stupid of me!

vocabulary.definition: Extreme fear.
vocabulary.example: He thought it was due to terror.

vocabulary.definition: To turn around rapidly; to spin.
vocabulary.example: He whirled around on Janet so fiercely that Janet half got out of her seat.

vocabulary.definition: The practice of magic, especially black magic or sorcery.
vocabulary.example: Michael took her witchcraft away last night.
